summary: Gruber dissects Twitter's confusing and short-lived timeline redesign that defaulted users to an algorithmic 'Home' feed while hiding the chronological 'Latest' timeline behind a cryptic sparkle icon. The feature lasted only four days before Twitter reversed course after user backlash. Gruber argues the design was deliberately hostile, making it difficult to find and maintain the chronological feed most long-time users prefer. He notes that even 'pinning' the Latest timeline didn't stick between app launches, forcing repeated swipes. While acknowledging Twitter had been shipping good features recently like Twitter Blue's Top Articles and bookmarks, he compares this move to trying to be Facebook. He closes by expressing surprise that more people don't use third-party clients like Tweetbot and Twitterrific, which show only chronological timelines by design.
key_points: Twitter's new timeline defaulted to an algorithmic feed and hid the chronological option behind an inscrutable sparkle icon, effectively removing the core Twitter experience for most users
Even users who discovered the 'pin' option had their preference reset every time they reopened the app, a design choice clearly meant to push algorithmic engagement over user preference
Despite this misstep, Twitter had been shipping genuinely good features like Twitter Blue's Top Articles and private bookmarks
Third-party clients like Tweetbot and Twitterrific offer a purely chronological, ad-free experience that many users seem unaware of
The sparkle icon choice felt like deliberate obfuscation with plausible deniability — hiding an option while claiming it was accessible
money_quotes: Not to be too cynical, but it feels like this icon choice was driven by a desire to hide this option while maintaining plausible deniability that the option had been hidden.
The whole thing felt like using a Twitter app in a bad dream where you can't figure out how anything works.
The only reason it worked this way is that Twitter clearly wants to push people to the algorithmic feed to increase aggregate engagement. To hell with what users say they want to see.
The default is to 'unpin' the tweets you want to see.
I would like to talk to the zookeeper that hired the baboon that designed this feature.
Twitter could change the ✨ icon to a 🍌 and it would make just as much, if not more, sense as the icon for that menu.

My last takeaway from Twitter's four-day venture into pretending to be Facebook (and that anyone who uses Twitter wants them to be Facebook) is that I'm surprised how many people clearly have no idea that excellent third-party Twitter clients exist.
You just pay a few bucks — the price of a beer or a sandwich — and apps like Tweetbot and Twitterrific make Twitter what you want Twitter to be.
key_insight: Twitter's timeline redesign was a textbook case of hostile design — hiding user preferences behind obscure UI while resetting choices on every launch to force-feed an algorithmic timeline nobody asked for.
